rules.description: Every broker must implement a local interface.
Brokers must contain no flow control (no if, switch, for, while).
Brokers must not handle exceptions — they propagate raw to the service.
Brokers own their own configuration — connection strings and credentials are injected into the broker only.
Brokers must use native C# primitives, not framework-specific types, in their method signatures where possible.
Brokers use infrastructure language (InsertStudentAsync) not business language (AddStudentAsync).
Brokers communicate upward to services and sideways to support brokers. Never to other entity brokers.
One broker wraps exactly one external resource.
All broker methods must return ValueTask or ValueTask<T> (prefer ValueTask over Task per §1.5.1). This includes SelectAll* — it must return ValueTask<IQueryable<T>>, never synchronous IQueryable<T>.

Asynchronization Abstraction (§1.5.1): every publicly exposed interface method — across brokers, services, and exposers — must return ValueTask or ValueTask<T>, even if the current implementation does not internally await anything. The public contract must remain uniformly async so callers never need to change when an implementation evolves from synchronous to asynchronous. Wrapping a synchronous result with ValueTask.FromResult<T>() or using the async keyword on an expression-bodied method are both acceptable.
Support brokers (Logging, DateTime, Identifier) provide cross-cutting infrastructure, not entity operations.
StorageBroker constructor must call this.Database.Migrate() to apply pending migrations at startup.

Entity partials must delegate to private generic helpers (InsertAsync<T>, SelectAllAsync<T>, SelectAsync<T>, UpdateAsync<T>, DeleteAsync<T>) defined in the base partial. Entity partials must never reference this.Entry(), this.Set<T>(), this.FindAsync(), this.SaveChangesAsync(), or DbSet properties directly.

Before scaffolding broker operations, the agent must clarify which specific operation(s) are required. A branch scoped to one operation (e.g., BROKERS-student-insert) must implement only that operation — not all CRUD.

DbSet<Entity> properties must be declared in the entity partial file (e.g., StorageBroker.Students.cs), never in the base StorageBroker.cs. The base partial owns only configuration, the constructor, OnConfiguring, and private generic helpers.
Foundation services are pure-primitive: input and output types are always the same entity type.
Foundation services integrate with exactly one entity type.
Foundation services use business language (Add, Retrieve, Modify, Remove) not infrastructure language.
Foundation services must validate all inputs before delegating to a broker.
Structural validations (null, empty, default) must run first and break immediately (circuit-breaking).
Logical validations (business rules) run after structural validations.
External validations (existence checks) run after logical validations.
Dependency validations cover broker-specific failure conditions.
Continuous validations must collect all invalid fields before throwing.
All exceptions from brokers must be caught, localized into Standard exceptions, categorized, and logged.
Exception categories: ValidationException, DependencyValidationException, CriticalDependencyException, DependencyException, ServiceException.
Foundation services must verify no unwanted broker calls occur after every operation.
Non-local models (external API models) must be mapped to local models before returning.
Processing services depend on exactly one foundation service.
Processing services validate only the data they actually use.
Processing services implement higher-order logic: Ensure, Upsert, TryAdd, TryRemove.
Processing services implement shifters: transformations from one type to another.
Processing services implement combinations: retrieve+add, retrieve+modify (upsert).
Processing services must unwrap foundation exceptions and re-wrap as processing-level exceptions.
Pass-through methods (no processing logic) are allowed and must delegate directly to the foundation service.
Orchestration services must follow the Florance Pattern: 2-3 service dependencies.
Orchestration services coordinate multi-entity flows across multiple services.
Call order must be enforced explicitly when flow correctness depends on it.
Natural order (enforced by input/output dependencies) is preferred over mock-sequence verification.
Orchestration services must unwrap and re-wrap exceptions from their dependencies.
Orchestration variations: Coordination (2-3 deps), Management (3+ deps), UberManagement (4+), Unit of Work.
Aggregation services must not validate dependency call order.
Aggregation services must not use mock-sequence style assertions.
Aggregation services perform basic structural validations only.
Aggregation services may pass-through to multiple service dependencies without ordering constraints.
Aggregation services must aggregate exceptions from all dependencies.
Exposers (controllers) contain no business logic — pure mapping only.
Each entity has a single exposure point (one controller per entity).
REST controller routes follow pattern: /api/[entities] (plural entity name).
HTTP success codes: 200 for GET/PUT/DELETE, 201 for POST.
Validation errors → 400. Dependency validation → 400. Critical dependency → 500. Dependency → 500. Service → 500.
Controllers must not catch exceptions — they map them via middleware or problem-details handler.
Services must never call other services at the same layer (no Foundation→Foundation calls).
Services must never call infrastructure directly — only through brokers.
Dependency flow is strictly: Exposer → Service(s) → Broker(s) → External Resource.
Every service type must be declared and tested independently.
